steps.title: Identify what the task requires
steps.content: The student wants to emphasize a difference in the origins of the two words. This means the correct choice must name both words' origins explicitly and contrast them.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the right one
steps.content:
• Choice A — '"Guerdon," the final word of the 2008 Scripps National Spelling Bee, is of Anglo-French origin, while the following year’s final word, "Laodicean," derives from ancient Greek.': Names Anglo-French origin for "guerdon" and ancient Greek for "Laodicean," using "while" to signal the contrast — hits both requirements. ✓
  • Choice B — 'In 2008, Sameer Mishra won the Scripps National Spelling Bee by correctly spelling the word "guerdon"; however, the following year, Kavya Shivashankar won based on spelling the word "Laodicean."': Compares the two winners but never mentions either word’s linguistic origin. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Kavya Shivashankar won the 2009 Scripps National Spelling Bee by correctly spelling "Laodicean," which derives from the ancient Greek word "Laodíkeia."': Gives the origin of only one word; omits "guerdon" entirely, so no contrast is possible. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The Scripps National Spelling Bee uses words from diverse linguistic origins, such as "guerdon" and "Laodicean."': Mentions both words but treats them as generic examples of diversity — it never specifies what each word’s origin is. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
